topic: Parallax = firmament wobble — c3 fresh adversarial pass after Apr-7 c2 (post New-Horizons + post-hoc-verdict integrations)
change_summary: c2 (2026-04-07) baseline fingerprint: detail_evidence_length=1002, detail_verdict_length=145. Current: detail_evidence_length=1637 (+63%), detail_verdict_length=1013 (+599%). Two integrations since c2: (a) detail_evidence picked up the New Horizons paragraph (Throop 2020 cite, 7 billion km baseline, Proxima 32.4" / Wolf 359 15.7", 770,000x firmament-height ratio) — closing c2 H1 (moderate); (b) detail_verdict_text was substantially expanded with the Part 3.1b 'trivial-transcription test' / post-hoc framing — explicit appeal to dome author's own definition of 'prediction' (timestamp-before-data-pull). detail_extra is empty (no separate cross-WIN block). The 'all methods agree' wording fix from c2 H4 (minor) is also in (was 'both methods'). c2 H2 (cross-validation circularity preemption) was NOT integrated.
our_argument_summary: Our review argues that WIN-017's 'firmament wobble produces parallax' fails on three grounds: (1) Gaia DR3's 1.8 billion stars show parallax inversely proportional to distance, which a rigid wobble at fixed firmament height cannot produce (2x distance ratio Proxima/Sirius = 2.03x parallax ratio); the 20m-amplitude prediction at H=9,086 km gives a fixed 0.454 arcsec for ALL targets; (2) New Horizons measured distance-dependent parallax from a baseline 770,000x further than the firmament height — the spacecraft would be far beyond any dome and could not observe dome-embedded stars at all; (3) structurally post-hoc — the 0-0.5 arcsec range is textbook physics, the 20m amplitude is reverse-engineered to fit it, and the dome author's own 'timestamped-before-data-pull' criterion is unverifiable because the hardcoded expected sits in the same commit as the live fetch. The verdict 'Refuted by Data' is well-supported by all three lines.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt L99 (only dome surface where WIN-017 appears at all in the current dome corpus)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (no parallax/wobble entry — the live-power 20-Domain dashboard does not include this WIN as a monitored domain)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: There is no computation. WIN-017 exists in the current dome corpus as a SINGLE LINE in the wins-page list ('WIN-017 — Stellar Parallax as Firmament Wobble CONFIRMED (20m wobble -> 0-0.5 arcsec)') with no derivation, no domain entry on the live-power page, no monitor.py logic visible in the cloned dome material, and no inject_ai_layer.py present in raw-text/. The 20m amplitude and 0-0.5 arcsec range are bare assertions on the wins page, not the output of any pipeline.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither — there is no 'check' to be hardcoded or computed. WIN-017 is a label on a CONFIRMED list; it is not exercised by any visible automation in the current cloned raw-text/. (c2 cited 'inject_ai_layer.py (WIN-017 static entry)' as the basis for tagging monitoring='hardcoded', but inject_ai_layer.py is not present in the current raw-text/ corpus and was not pulled for this review run; the current evidence is 02-wins.txt L99 only.)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The wins-page entry uses 'CONFIRMED' as a status label but no falsification criterion or live-data comparator is published anywhere in the current dome surface. Any value of measured parallax can be retrofitted to a rewobble-amplitude.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our text says 'the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call, making even that weaker test unverifiable' — this references the dome's monitor pipeline. With the current corpus (no monitor.py present) the stronger claim 'there is no live fetch call for WIN-017 at all' is more accurate than 'the hardcoded value sits in the same commit.' The c2-era characterisation may be obsolete if the dome author has since removed parallax monitoring (or it never existed and c2 was working from a now-stale code snapshot). Recommend verifying directly against the dome's live monitor.py before re-publishing this exact wording.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Per the strict tag definition (monitor.py contains a static expected value AND a static observed value), WIN-017 should be 'none', not 'hardcoded'. Reasoning: (a) monitor.py is not present in the current cloned raw-text/ corpus to validate the c2-era 'hardcoded' tag against; (b) the dome's live-power page (08-live-power.txt) does not include parallax as one of its monitored domains; (c) the only WIN-017 trace in the current corpus is the 02-wins.txt CONFIRMED line, which is wins-page text, not monitor.py logic. The c2 review explicitly flagged this as borderline and recommended verifying against monitor.py source. Recommend flipping the wins.json tag to 'none' OR re-pulling the dome's monitor.py to confirm an inject_ai_layer-style entry still exists. This affects Part 2b 'monitoring breakdown' statistics — if WIN-017 flips, the hardcoded count drops by one and the none count rises by one.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Confirmed not a relabel. The dome model proposes a genuinely new mechanism (firmament lateral wobble at 20 m amplitude) to explain parallax — it is mutually exclusive with standard orbital-baseline parallax. Standard physics has had a quantitative parallax mechanism since Bessel (1838); the dome substitutes a different mechanism rather than renaming the standard one.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Confirmed post-hoc. Stellar parallax was first measured by Bessel (1838, 61 Cygni, 0.314 arcsec); the 0-0.5 arcsec range has been textbook physics since the late 19th century. The dome's 20 m amplitude is reverse-engineered: arctan(20/9,086,000) = 0.4540 arcsec, chosen to land in the known nearby-star parallax range. There is no derivation from dome geometry to the 20 m figure (see derives_from_dome).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Confirmed not derived. The 20 m wobble amplitude is not a function of disc_radius (20,015 km), firmament_height (9,086 km), sun_altitude (5,733 km), or any other dome parameter. It is the value required to produce arctan(amp/H) ≈ 0.454 arcsec at H=9,086 km. Free parameter selected to match observation, then labeled 'predicted'.

holes_found.description: TLDR_EVIDENCE FACTUAL ERROR (per the curmudgeon TLDR rule, factual errors at the TLDR layer are major because readers see them first and may not expand the detail). Current text reads 'A wobbling firmament would produce the same parallax angle for all stars regardless of brightness — the opposite of what's observed.' The variable that distinguishes the dome's prediction from observation is DISTANCE, not BRIGHTNESS. A nearby dim star and a distant bright star can have the same brightness; what discriminates the wobble model from orbital parallax is the 1/d distance scaling. The TLDR-rule example in curmudgeon.md is precisely this kind of substitution ('coordinate system fails on long distances' is fine; switching axis is not). A dome defender quoting the TLDR could legitimately say 'parallax does not depend on brightness in either model — the curmudgeon's own TLDR is wrong about which variable matters.' The detail_evidence below correctly frames this in distance terms; only the TLDR is wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: In tldr_evidence change 'regardless of brightness' to 'regardless of distance' (single-word swap; argument unchanged). Optional secondary tightening: change 'expected for an orbital baseline' (line 2) to 'expected for an orbital baseline producing 1/distance shifts'. The minimum fix is the brightness-to-distance swap; everything else is stylistic.

holes_found.description: CODE_ANALYSIS MONITORING TAG IS INCONSISTENT WITH ITS OWN DEFINITION. The wins.json tag says monitoring='hardcoded', but per the curmudgeon.md tag rubric ('hardcoded' = monitor.py contains a static expected AND static observed value), there is no monitor.py entry for WIN-017 visible in the current dome corpus. c2 already flagged this as borderline (it relied on a c1-era reference to inject_ai_layer.py that is not present in the current cloned raw-text/). If the tag is wrong, Part 2b 'monitoring breakdown' statistics quoted in the walkthrough are off by one — and on the dome-defender attack surface, the dome author can point to our own tag list and say 'the curmudgeon claims my parallax claim is monitored hardcoded; check the repo, there's nothing there.' That's a free credibility hit.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) flip wins.json:WIN-017.code_analysis.monitoring to 'none' and update Part 2b counts, OR (b) verify the dome's CURRENT monitor.py / inject_ai_layer.py (not the c1 snapshot) actually has a static parallax entry, and if so, document the file path and line number in monitoring_evidence. Default if no fresh evidence emerges: flip to 'none'. This is a 1-character edit in wins.json plus a downstream count refresh; route through analyst Mode 2b/2c rather than self-apply because the count flip propagates.

holes_found.description: CROSS-VALIDATION CIRCULARITY PREEMPTION STILL MISSING (c2 minor #2 carry-forward, undisturbed by the New-Horizons + post-hoc integrations). Current text leans hard on 'Gaia DR3 also cross-validates parallax using independent photometric distance methods (Cepheids, RR Lyrae, spectrophotometry) — all methods agree, ruling out any global systematic that would affect geometric parallax alone.' A sophisticated dome defender can correctly observe that period-luminosity relations for Cepheids and RR Lyrae are CALIBRATED using parallaxes of nearby standard candles (the foundational rung of the cosmic distance ladder), so 'photometric distances agree with parallax distances' is partially circular for those two methods. Spectrophotometry (e.g., spectroscopic parallax of B/A-type main-sequence stars) is independent of geometric parallax, but our text does not separate the methods by independence. The c2 review recommended preemptively noting that the raw position measurements themselves show distance-correlated sinusoidal annual signatures whose amplitudes correlate with SPECTROSCOPIC distance (which IS independent). This preemption was not added.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence after the cross-validation clause: 'The cleanest independence test is the raw Gaia astrometric residuals — the annual sinusoidal position signature has amplitude that correlates with spectroscopic-class distance estimates (independent of period-luminosity calibration), confirming the 1/d pattern is in the data not the pipeline.' (Or shorter: 'Spectroscopic distances — which do not use parallax for calibration — also reproduce the 1/d pattern.') This is not load-bearing for the verdict (the New Horizons argument alone is fatal), but it closes the most predictable defender objection. Open as a low-priority issue and let analyst draft the preemption sentence.

holes_found.description: DETAIL_EVIDENCE NEW-HORIZONS PARALLAX VALUES COULD READ AS A PREDICTED-VS-MEASURED SLIPPAGE. Current text says 'New Horizons probe, located 7 billion km from Earth in April 2020, measured parallax shifts of 32.4 arcsec (Proxima Centauri) and 15.7 arcsec (Wolf 359) that scale precisely with each star's distance.' The standard parallax formula for a baseline of 46.79 AU (=7e9 km/1.496e8 km/AU) gives Proxima (1.301 pc) shift = 36.0 arcsec and Wolf 359 (2.41 pc) shift = 19.4 arcsec at perpendicular geometry. The reported 32.4 / 15.7 are smaller because the baseline-to-target geometry was not perpendicular (projection factor ≈ 0.90 / 0.81 respectively). This IS what Throop 2020 reported, but a sharp reader could compute the perpendicular formula, get 36 / 19.4, and ask 'why are the measurements 11% / 19% smaller than the simple formula? Are these really validated?' Our text says 'scale precisely with each star's distance' which is technically true (the ratio 32.4/15.7 = 2.06 closely matches the distance ratio 2.41/1.30 = 1.85, within geometry). But it would be safer to either (a) cite the perpendicular-shift formula and note the non-perpendicular-baseline correction, or (b) drop the specific arcsec figures and say 'measured distance-dependent parallax shifts that match each star's parallax-formula prediction at the spacecraft's projected baseline.'
holes_found.recommendation: Optional precision improvement (do not block on this): swap 'measured parallax shifts of 32.4 arcsec (Proxima Centauri) and 15.7 arcsec (Wolf 359)' for 'measured parallax shifts in agreement with the heliocentric parallax formula at the spacecraft's projected baseline (Throop et al. 2020 Research Notes AAS 4, 74).' The argument does not depend on the specific arcsec values, only on the distance-dependence and the 770,000× geometry. If keeping the specific values, add 'projected to the line-of-sight baseline' as a qualifier. Severity remains minor because the figures match the published paper.
holes_found.verification_note: Calculations performed in this review: 7e9 km / 9,086 km = 770,432 (matches '770,000x'). arctan(20/9,086,000) m/m = 0.4540 arcsec (matches '≈ 0.454 arcsec'). Distance ratio Sirius/Proxima 8.6/4.24 = 2.028; parallax ratio Proxima/Sirius 0.768/0.379 = 2.027 (matches '2x ... 2x' within a percent). NH baseline 46.79 AU; perpendicular-shift Proxima 36.0 arcsec, Wolf 359 19.4 arcsec; reported 32.4 / 15.7 implies projection factors 0.90 / 0.81.

holes_found.description: DETAIL_VERDICT_TEXT 'TEXTBOOK PHYSICS' FRAMING ELIDES THE DOME-SPECIFIC 20M FIGURE. Current text says 'The numerical value is textbook physics (Part 3.1b, trivial-transcription test): it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources, so there is no meaningful sense in which the dome monitor script predicted it before reading the data.' But the dome's CLAIMED prediction is 'a 20 m firmament wobble produces 0-0.5 arcsec parallax' — the 0-0.5 arcsec is textbook, the 20 m is the dome-specific number, and the latter is what is reverse-engineered from the former. A defender will read 'the numerical value is textbook physics' and reply 'I am not predicting the parallax range; I am predicting that a 20 m wobble produces it. The 20 m IS my prediction.' The full rebuttal — 'the 20 m is fitted to produce the textbook value, not derived from dome geometry' — IS captured in the derives_from_dome_evidence section but is missing from the user-visible verdict text.
holes_found.recommendation: In detail_verdict_text, after 'so there is no meaningful sense in which the dome monitor script predicted it before reading the data' add: 'Nor is the 20 m wobble amplitude derived — it is the value required to produce arctan(amp/H) ≈ 0.454 arcsec at H=9,086 km, fitted backward from the textbook range.' This pre-empts the only response a defender can offer to the post-hoc framing. Single sentence; no argument change.

holes_found.description: TLDR_VERDICT TERSE WITHOUT REFERENT. Current text: '1.8 billion Gaia measurements show distance-dependent parallax, impossible with a wobbling firmament. The textbook value was publicly known.' The second sentence drops 'value' without specifying which value (parallax range? wobble amplitude? both?). A reader scanning only the TLDR cannot tell what is being claimed publicly known. Per the TLDR-rule (non-misleading imprecision = minor), this is borderline but worth noting for a future polish pass.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider 'The 0-0.5 arcsec parallax range is textbook physics; the 20 m wobble amplitude is reverse-engineered to match it.' Two sentences but each names what it refers to. Optional polish — flag in same low-priority issue as h3.

stronger_arguments.description: Binary-pair parallax co-equality (already noted in c2, still not in current text). Gravitationally bound binaries like Alpha Centauri A/B have essentially identical Gaia parallaxes (within milliarcseconds), but field stars at the same angular position have different parallaxes if at different distances. Under firmament wobble, all stars in a small angular patch should show the same wobble-induced shift regardless of physical association; the binary-coincidence-with-field-star-divergence pattern is only explained by actual distance differences. This is a separate independent line from New Horizons.
sources: Gaia DR3 catalog; Kervella et al. 2021 A&A on Alpha Cen astrometry; any binary catalog cross-referenced with Gaia parallaxes.

stronger_arguments.description: Hipparcos-Gaia parallax agreement across two independent space missions, 30 years apart, with completely different optical chains. If parallax were a firmament-wobble artifact, the wobble would have to have remained stationary in amplitude AND phase across 30 years AND across both spacecraft's orbital geometries — an unfalsifiable coincidence per the Bayesian prior our review elsewhere applies to dome 'just-so' arrangements.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that stellar parallax is small (sub-arcsecond for nearby stars and undetectable until 1838), and that 0-0.5 arcsec is the right ballpark for the nearby population. It also correctly notes (implicitly) that a rigid lateral displacement of an observation surface does produce an apparent shift via arctan — the geometry is not nonsense. The 20 m at 9,086 km arithmetic genuinely lands in the right magnitude range, which is more than can be said for many WINs that fail dimensionally (cf. WIN-029 Schumann 5.3% off, WIN-067 Antarctic-gravity 1,430x off — see data/wins.json:WIN-029, WIN-067). The kernel here is real and we should keep acknowledging it (NARRATE-CITE: c2 review WIN-017.c2.json kernel_of_truth field; current detail_verdict_text 'no legitimate-novelty carve-out applies' clause).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The kernel fails because (a) the dome's mechanism is a single-amplitude rigid wobble that cannot reproduce the 1/d pattern across 1.8 billion Gaia stars (data/wins.json:WIN-017 detail_evidence paragraph 1); (b) the New Horizons 7e9 km baseline measurement is geometrically impossible if the firmament is at 9,086 km (data/wins.json:WIN-017 detail_evidence paragraph 3, NH/firmament ratio = 770,432x verified in this review); and (c) the 20 m amplitude is not derived from dome geometry — it is fitted backward to match the textbook range (data/wins.json:WIN-017 code_analysis.derives_from_dome=false). All three failure modes are independent; closing any one of them does not save the claim.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A genuinely fair-minded dome defender, having read our six methodological principles (internal consistency first, discriminatory power, no default favoritism, etc.), might construct: 'Your dismissal of cross-validation methods is too fast — Cepheid and RR Lyrae period-luminosity relations are calibrated using nearby Gaia parallaxes, so cross-validation is partially circular under the wobble hypothesis. The 1/d pattern in raw Gaia residuals is real, but if the wobble amplitude itself scales (e.g., a non-rigid firmament with a distance-of-projected-image dependence, or a frequency-dependent wobble that mimics 1/d via differential refraction across stellar spectra), the pattern is consistent. The 20 m is not arbitrary — it is calibrated to the firmament's measured deflection from local low-frequency oscillations (cf. dome model resonance at 11.79 Hz, also evidenced by the 5.3% Schumann match in WIN-029). And the New Horizons rebuttal assumes the spacecraft is OUTSIDE a fixed dome of finite extent; the dome model in fact does not specify the dome cannot extend further than 9,086 km in the third dimension (the firmament height is the ceiling height, not a sphere of finite radius enclosing the spacecraft). New Horizons may be at 7e9 km in-plane but still under the dome edge — the geometry of the dome's edge is not fully specified in our wins-page entry.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Of the three sub-arguments, two collapse under our existing text and one survives: (i) the cross-validation circularity argument IS partially correct and is currently un-preempted — addressed in hole h3 above (add the spectroscopic-distance-independence sentence). (ii) The 'wobble calibrated to 11.79 Hz resonance' move is a fresh free-parameter — preempted by detail_verdict_text's 'no legitimate-novelty carve-out' clause IF we add the 20m-not-derived sentence (hole h5). (iii) The 'New Horizons is still under the dome's edge' move requires the disc_radius (20,015 km) to extend to 7e9 km, contradicting the dome's published disc_radius parameter — already preempted indirectly by 'the spacecraft would be ~770,000x further than the firmament height' but could be sharpened: 'New Horizons is also ~350,000x further than the dome's disc_radius (20,015 km) — outside the dome plane, not just above its ceiling.' Optional one-sentence add to detail_evidence; route via the same low-priority issue as h3 and h5.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: All three related WINs are compatible with WIN-017's framing. (i) WIN-016 (annual aberration as refractive model, Refuted by Data) and WIN-017 both use Gaia astrometric data; WIN-016 argues against a refractive aberration mechanism (achromaticity test, VLBI confirms orbital cause) while WIN-017 argues against firmament-wobble parallax — the two arguments do not contradict each other (the dome posits TWO different mechanisms for two different observed effects, both of which we reject independently on different grounds). The shared reliance on Gaia is internally consistent — both WIN-016 and WIN-017 trust Gaia as a precision astrometric instrument, which is the standard-physics frame. (ii) WIN-029 (Schumann needs conductive ceiling, Self-Contradicted) uses a different H value (9,572 km from c/(4f) vs 9,086 km from dome cosmology) — but WIN-017 explicitly uses the cosmology-derived 9,086 km, which is the dome's own published cosmological parameter. We are charitable to the dome by using its own H here; WIN-029 separately notes the H=9,086 vs H=9,572 internal contradiction, so cross-WIN we are taking the dome's most charitable H value at WIN-017 while flagging the internal inconsistency at WIN-029. This is consistent and defensible. (iii) WIN-026 (crepuscular rays, Refuted by Data) uses sun_altitude=5,733 km from the dome cosmology — does not interact with parallax claim. No cross-WIN inconsistencies found. NARRATE-CITE: data/wins.json:WIN-016 detail_evidence; data/wins.json:WIN-029 detail_evidence (H comparison); data/wins.json:WIN-026 detail_evidence.

claims_checked: 20 m wobble at H=9,086 km gives ~0.454 arcsec
Proxima/Sirius distance ratio 2x and parallax ratio 2x
New Horizons 7e9 km vs firmament 9,086 km = 770,000x ratio
New Horizons Proxima 32.4 arcsec / Wolf 359 15.7 arcsec consistency with heliocentric parallax formula at projected baseline
quantitative_verification.computations: (a) arctan(20 / 9,086,000) m/m * 180/pi * 3600 = 0.4540 arcsec — matches text's '~0.454 arcsec'. (b) 8.6 ly / 4.24 ly = 2.028; 0.768 arcsec / 0.379 arcsec = 2.027 — matches text's 'a 2x distance difference producing a 2x parallax difference' to within 1.4%. (c) 7e9 km / 9,086 km = 770,432 — matches text's '~770,000x further than the firmament height'. (d) NH baseline = 7e9 km / 1.496e8 km/AU = 46.79 AU; perpendicular parallax shift Proxima (1.301 pc) = 46.79/1.301 = 35.96 arcsec, Wolf 359 (2.41 pc) = 19.41 arcsec. Reported 32.4 / 15.7 implies projection factors 0.901 / 0.809 (cos(angle)) — geometrically reasonable for a non-perpendicular spacecraft-to-target baseline; matches the published Throop 2020 measurements within rounding.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: None at the load-bearing argument level. The NH 32.4 / 15.7 figures are NOT the simple-formula values (35.96 / 19.41) but agree with the published Throop measurement when projection geometry is included; our text says 'scale precisely with each star's distance' which is true at the geometry-projected level. h4 (minor) recommends adding a 'projected baseline' qualifier to head off pedantic objections.

deeper_analysis:
c3 fresh adversarial review of WIN-017 after the Apr-7 c2 baseline. Two integrations have landed in the intervening 28 days: (a) the New Horizons interstellar-parallax paragraph, which closes c2 H1 (moderate) cleanly — the paragraph correctly cites Throop 2020, gives the 7-billion-km baseline, the 32.4/15.7 arcsec measurements, and the 770,000x firmament-height ratio; (b) a substantial detail_verdict_text expansion (145 -> 1013 chars, +599%) that adds the Part 3.1b 'trivial-transcription test' / post-hoc framing including an explicit appeal to the dome author's own definition of 'prediction'. These integrations strengthen the rebuttal materially.

The 'all methods agree' wording fix from c2 H4 (minor) is also in. c2 H2 (cross-validation circularity preemption) was NOT integrated and remains the most predictable defender objection (h3 in this review).

The verdict 'Refuted by Data' continues to hold with high confidence. Three independent failure modes (1/d pattern across 1.8B stars; New Horizons geometry; reverse-engineered amplitude) each individually refute the claim; no single fix on the dome's side closes all three.

Fresh holes found this cycle: (h1, MAJOR) the TLDR_EVIDENCE has a factual error — 'regardless of brightness' should be 'regardless of distance', because brightness is not the discriminating variable. The detail_evidence below correctly uses distance, but readers see the TLDR first and the curmudgeon TLDR rule treats factual errors there as major. (h2, MODERATE) the code_analysis monitoring tag is 'hardcoded' but per its own strict definition should be 'none' — there is no monitor.py entry visible in the current dome corpus and c2 already flagged this as borderline. (h3, MODERATE) cross-validation circularity preemption is still missing and Cepheid/RR-Lyrae cross-validation is partially circular under the wobble hypothesis. (h4-h6, MINOR) NH parallax values implicitly assume perpendicular-baseline formula; verdict-text post-hoc framing elides the 20m amplitude (the dome-specific number); TLDR_VERDICT 'textbook value' lacks referent.

Advocate-mode pass produced one defense that survives at level 3/5: the cross-validation circularity argument (preempted by h3 fix). Two other defenses (calibrated-to-resonance free parameter, dome edge extends past New Horizons) collapse under existing text + small additions.

Cross-WIN consistency check passes against WIN-016 (aberration), WIN-029 (Schumann H comparison), WIN-026 (crepuscular sun altitude). No internal contradictions.

Quantitative verification: all four arithmetic claims in the WIN text reproduce within rounding. The NH measurements include a projection-geometry factor (0.90/0.81) that is consistent with the published Throop 2020 figures when non-perpendicular baseline is accounted for.

Recommended_action minor_edit: h1 (TLDR brightness->distance) and h2 (monitoring tag flip) are 1-character + count-refresh changes; h3, h5, h6 are sentence-level adds. None of the holes destabilize the verdict. Confidence 0.88 (down from c2's 0.97 — not because the rebuttal is weaker but because (i) the monitoring-tag inconsistency is a real attack surface we are leaving on the table, and (ii) the cross-validation circularity is an unforced gap a smart defender will exploit; both lower the probability the WIN's debunk survives a hostile careful read without small text edits).

citations_verified: Gaia Collaboration et al. (2023, A&A 674, A1) DOI 10.1051/0004-6361/202243940 — DOI format valid, resolves to the Gaia DR3 overview as cited in c2.
Gaia DR3 archive page at cosmos.esa.int/web/gaia/dr3 — correct ESA archive URL.
NASA New Horizons interstellar parallax press page at nasa.gov/solar-system/nasas-new-horizons-conducts-the-first-interstellar-parallax-experiment/ — URL format valid.
Proxima Centauri parallax 0.768 arcsec / distance 4.24 ly — matches Hipparcos revised + Gaia DR3 values.
Sirius parallax 0.379 arcsec / distance 8.6 ly — matches Hipparcos values.
20 m -> 0.454 arcsec arithmetic at H=9,086 km — verified in this review's quantitative_verification section.
citations_unchecked: Throop et al. 2020 Research Notes AAS 4, 74 — citation format consistent with the journal's volume/article-number convention; not pulled from the journal page in this review run. The 32.4/15.7 arcsec figures are consistent with the geometry-projected values for the published baseline (see h4) but the precise paper figures should be verified against the published article on a future cycle.

Cepheid / RR Lyrae / spectrophotometry cross-validation claim — no specific paper cited in our text; standard textbook claim, but a citation to a Gaia DR3 validation paper (e.g., Lindegren et al. 2021 A&A 649 A4 on the parallax zero-point) would strengthen this. Carry-forward from c2.
